Definition: The broad field of computer science focused on creating systems capable of performing tasks that typically require human intelligence such as reasoning learning and problem-solving.
A subset of AI where algorithms learn patterns from data to make predictions or decisions without being explicitly programmed.
A subset of machine learning that uses artificial neural networks with multiple layers (deep neural networks) to model complex patterns in data.
A type of ML where the model is trained on labeled data (input-output pairs) to learn a mapping function. Examples: classification and regression.
A type of ML where the model finds hidden patterns or structures in unlabeled data. Examples: clustering and dimensionality reduction.
A type of ML where an agent learns to make decisions by taking actions in an environment and receiving rewards or penalties.
A learning approach that combines a small amount of labeled data with a large amount of unlabeled data during training.
The dataset used to teach a machine learning model to recognize patterns and make predictions.
A subset of data used during training to tune hyperparameters and evaluate model performance to prevent overfitting.
A separate dataset used after training to evaluate the final performance of a model on unseen data.
An individual measurable property or characteristic of the data used as input to a machine learning model.
The target output or answer associated with each training example in supervised learning.
A mathematical representation learned from data that can make predictions or decisions on new unseen data.
The process of using a trained model to make predictions on new data.
When a model learns the training data too well (including noise) resulting in poor performance on new unseen data.
When a model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns in the data resulting in poor performance on both training and test data.
Systematic error introduced by assumptions in the learning algorithm that cause it to miss relevant relations between features and target outputs.
The amount by which the model's predictions would change if trained on a different dataset; high variance indicates overfitting.
A configuration setting external to the model that is set before training begins (e.g. learning rate or number of layers).
One complete pass through the entire training dataset during the training process.
A computing system inspired by biological neural networks consisting of interconnected nodes (neurons) organized in layers that process information.
A deep learning architecture primarily used for image recognition and computer vision tasks using convolutional layers to detect spatial features.
A neural network architecture designed for sequential data (e.g. text and time series) where outputs from previous steps are fed as inputs to the current step.
A neural network architecture that uses self-attention mechanisms to process sequential data in parallel forming the basis of modern LLMs like GPT and BERT.
A technique where a model trained on one task is reused as the starting point for a model on a different but related task.
AI systems that can create new content (text images code audio) based on patterns learned from training data.
A type of generative AI model trained on massive text datasets that can understand and generate human-like text.
A large AI model trained on broad data at scale that can be adapted (fine-tuned) to a wide range of downstream tasks.
The input text or instruction given to a generative AI model to guide its output.
The practice of designing and refining input prompts to get desired outputs from generative AI models.
The process of further training a pre-trained model on a smaller task-specific dataset to improve performance on that task.
A technique that enhances LLM responses by retrieving relevant information from external knowledge sources before generating an answer.
When a generative AI model produces outputs that are factually incorrect fabricated or not grounded in the input data.
A parameter that controls the randomness of a model's output; lower values produce more deterministic responses and higher values produce more creative ones.
The basic units of text (words subwords or characters) that LLMs process as input and generate as output.
The maximum number of tokens an LLM can process in a single input/output interaction.
A numerical vector representation of data (text or images) that captures semantic meaning enabling similarity comparisons.
A database optimized for storing and querying high-dimensional vector embeddings commonly used in RAG architectures.
A technique used to fine-tune LLMs by incorporating human preferences and feedback into the training process.
A fully managed AWS service for building training and deploying machine learning models at scale.
A fully managed AWS service that provides access to foundation models from Amazon and third-party providers via a single API.
An AI-powered assistant by AWS that can answer questions generate content and take actions using enterprise data and systems.
An AWS service that provides pre-trained computer vision capabilities for image and video analysis (object detection and facial analysis etc.).
An AWS NLP service that uses ML to find insights and relationships in text including sentiment analysis entity recognition and topic modeling.
An AWS service that converts speech to text using automatic speech recognition (ASR).
An AWS service that converts text into lifelike speech using deep learning (text-to-speech).
An AWS neural machine translation service for translating text between languages.
An AWS service that automatically extracts text handwriting and structured data from scanned documents.
An AWS service for building conversational interfaces (chatbots) using voice and text powered by the same technology as Alexa.
An AWS intelligent search service powered by ML that provides natural language search across enterprise data.
An AWS service that enables developers to create real-time personalized recommendations using ML.
An AWS service that uses ML to deliver highly accurate time-series forecasts.
An AI-powered code generation assistant that provides real-time code suggestions and security scanning.
Custom AWS chips designed to deliver high-performance cost-effective ML training in the cloud.
Custom AWS chips designed to deliver high-performance low-cost ML inference in the cloud.
A feature of SageMaker that provides pre-trained models solution templates and example notebooks to accelerate ML development.
The practice of designing developing and deploying AI systems in a way that is ethical fair transparent and accountable.
Unfair or prejudiced outcomes produced by AI systems often resulting from biased training data or flawed model design.
The principle that AI systems should treat all individuals and groups equitably without discrimination.
The ability to understand and explain how an AI model arrives at its predictions or decisions.
The principle that AI systems should be open about how they work what data they use and their limitations.
The ability to identify harmful offensive or inappropriate content generated by or input to AI systems.
Configurable safeguards in Amazon Bedrock that filter harmful content block denied topics and redact sensitive information from model inputs/outputs.
An approach where humans review validate or override AI decisions to ensure accuracy and accountability.
The steps taken to clean transform and prepare raw data before feeding it into a machine learning model.
The process of creating selecting or transforming input features to improve model performance.
Techniques to artificially increase the size and diversity of training data (e.g. rotating images or paraphrasing text).
A data pipeline process that extracts data from sources transforms it into a usable format and loads it into a target system.
The proportion of correct predictions out of total predictions; commonly used for classification tasks.
The proportion of true positive predictions out of all positive predictions; measures how many selected items are relevant.
The proportion of true positive predictions out of all actual positives; measures how many relevant items are selected.
The harmonic mean of precision and recall providing a single metric that balances both.
Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve; measures a classification model's ability to distinguish between classes.
A metric for evaluating the quality of machine-generated text by comparing it to reference text; commonly used in translation.
A metric for evaluating language models; lower perplexity indicates the model is better at predicting the next token.
A set of metrics for evaluating text summarization by comparing overlap between generated and reference summaries.
The protection of personal and sensitive information from unauthorized access use or disclosure in AI systems.
Any data that can be used to identify a specific individual such as name SSN or email address.
The process of converting data into a coded format to prevent unauthorized access used to protect data at rest and in transit.
AWS service for securely controlling access to AWS resources by managing users groups roles and permissions.
Adherence to laws regulations and industry standards governing the use of AI and data (e.g. GDPR and HIPAA).
A security framework where AWS manages security OF the cloud (infrastructure) and customers manage security IN the cloud (data access and applications).
A field of AI focused on enabling computers to understand interpret and generate human language.
A field of AI that enables computers to interpret and understand visual information from images and videos.
An NLP technique that identifies and extracts the emotional tone (positive negative or neutral) from text.
An NLP task that identifies and classifies named entities (people organizations and locations) in text.
An unsupervised learning technique that groups similar data points together based on their features.
A supervised learning task where the model predicts a discrete category or class label for each input.
A supervised learning task where the model predicts a continuous numerical value.
Techniques to reduce the number of input features while preserving important information (e.g. PCA and t-SNE).
Processing a large volume of data through a model all at once rather than in real-time.
Processing data through a model immediately as it arrives providing instant predictions.
The degradation of model performance over time as the statistical properties of the input data change.
A method of comparing two versions of a model or system to determine which performs better with real users.
A set of practices that combines ML DevOps and data engineering to deploy and maintain ML systems in production reliably.
A SageMaker feature that continuously monitors deployed models for data quality model quality bias and feature attribution drift.
A SageMaker feature that helps detect bias in data and models and provides explanations for model predictions.
An AWS service that makes it easy to build human review workflows for ML predictions.
A type of generative model that creates data (often images) by learning to reverse a gradual noising process.
A generative model architecture consisting of two neural networks (generator and discriminator) that compete to produce realistic data.
The ability of a model to perform a task it was not explicitly trained on using only a task description or prompt.
The ability of a model to learn a new task from only a few examples provided in the prompt.
A prompt engineering technique that encourages the model to break down complex reasoning into intermediate steps.
AI systems that can autonomously plan reason and take actions using tools and APIs to accomplish complex tasks.
AI models that can process and generate multiple types of data (text images audio and video) simultaneously.
Amazon's family of foundation models available through Amazon Bedrock including text generation and embedding models.
A family of large language models by Anthropic available through Amazon Bedrock known for safety and helpfulness.
The process of breaking text into smaller units (tokens) that can be processed by an NLP or language model.
The time delay between sending a request to an AI model and receiving a response; critical for real-time applications.
The number of inference requests a model can process per unit of time.
Strategies to reduce the cost of training and deploying AI models such as using spot instances right-sizing or serverless inference.
